# Ion AI Agent Notes

Ion is a Rust embeddable scripting language. The main crate is `ion-core`;
the public embedding guide is `docs/embedding.md`; host-name hashing details
are in `docs/hide-names.md`; Python-style argument binding is documented in
`PYTHON_STYLE_ARGS_DESIGN.md`.

## Current Argument Model

Ion supports Python-style calls for Ion functions and signed host callables:
positional arguments, defaults, named arguments, `*args`, `**kwargs`,
positional-only parameters, and keyword-only parameters.

Implementation entry points:

- AST shapes: `ion-core/src/ast.rs` (`ParamKind`, `CallArgKind`).
- Parser and compiler: `ion-core/src/parser.rs`, `ion-core/src/compiler.rs`.
- Shared resolver: `ion-core/src/call.rs`.
- Runtime paths: `ion-core/src/interpreter.rs`, `ion-core/src/vm.rs`,
  and `ion-core/src/async_runtime.rs` with `async-runtime`.
- Host APIs: `ion-core/src/engine.rs`, `ion-core/src/module.rs`,
  and `ion-core/src/value.rs`.

## Host Integration Instructions

Use `ion_core::h!("name")` for host-visible identifiers. Do not introduce
string-keyed host registration APIs unless there is an explicit design change;
release builds are intended to keep host API names out of `.rodata`.

Unsigned host APIs are positional-only and reject named arguments:

```rust
engine.register_fn(h!("legacy"), legacy_fn);
engine.register_closure(h!("legacy_closure"), move |args| { ... });
engine.register_async_fn(h!("legacy_async"), |args| async move { ... });
```

When Ion scripts should call a host function with names, defaults, variadic
arguments, or keyword-only parameters, register a `HostSignature`:

```rust
use ion_core::{h, engine::Engine};
use ion_core::value::{HostArgs, HostSignature, Value};

let mut engine = Engine::new();

let sig = HostSignature::builder()
    .pos_required(h!("url"))
    .kw_only(h!("timeout_ms"), Value::Int(1000))
    .kw_only(h!("retries"), Value::Int(2))
    .build();
let sig_for_cb = sig.clone();

engine.register_closure_sig(h!("fetch"), sig, move |values| {
    let args = HostArgs::new(values, &sig_for_cb);
    let url = args.get_str(h!("url"))?;
    let timeout_ms = args.get(h!("timeout_ms")).and_then(Value::as_int).unwrap();
    let retries = args.get(h!("retries")).and_then(Value::as_int).unwrap();
    Ok(Value::Str(format!("{url}:{timeout_ms}:{retries}")))
});
```

Ion callers should then prefer named arguments over option dictionaries:

```ion
fetch("https://example.com", timeout_ms: 2500, retries: 4)
```

Prefer the named-argument style above instead of:

```ion
fetch(#{url: "https://example.com", timeout_ms: 2500, retries: 4})
```

Use dictionaries only for true map payloads, open-ended data, or values that
the script passes through as data. For fixed host API parameters, named
arguments give Ion's resolver the chance to validate missing, duplicate, and
unknown parameters and to apply host defaults consistently.

`Engine` and `Module` mirror the same signature-aware APIs:

- `register_fn_sig`
- `register_closure_sig`
- `register_async_fn_sig` with the `async-runtime` feature

`HostSignature::builder()` methods:

- `pos_required(h!("name"))`
- `pos(h!("name"), default_value)`
- `pos_only_required(h!("name"))`
- `pos_only(h!("name"), default_value)`
- `kw_only_required(h!("name"))`
- `kw_only(h!("name"), default_value)`
- `var_args(h!("args"))`
- `var_kwargs(h!("kwargs"))`

Callbacks still receive resolved values in signature declaration order. For
small callbacks, direct indexing is fine. Use `HostArgs::new(values, &sig)` for
clear name-based access inside closures that can capture the signature.

`**kwargs` values are `Value::Dict(IndexMap<String, Value>)`. Host parameter
matching hashes the Ion source keyword with `h(source_name)` and compares it to
the stored `HostParam::name_hash`.

## Verification Commands

After changing argument binding or host integration behavior, run at least:

```sh
cargo check -p ion-core
cargo test -p ion-core
```

If the change touches async host calls or async VM paths, also run:

```sh
cargo check -p ion-core --features async-runtime
cargo test -p ion-core --features async-runtime
```
